rationale: I split Ecology & Evolution into 14 practitioner-facing subspecialties to balance breadth (populations → global patterns) with enough granularity so a paper typically maps to one card. I kept classic disciplinary names ecologists and evolutionary biologists actually use (e.g., Population Ecology, Paleontology, Phylogenetics) and separated process- vs. scale-focused areas (Community vs. Ecosystem vs. Landscape). I deliberately did not create separate genomics or molecular subfields — those belong to Biology — and I avoided splitting highly overlapping applied areas (Conservation vs. Restoration) too finely while still giving them distinct slots. A few borderline splits (Macroecology vs. Biogeography, Invasion Biology vs. Community Ecology) were handled by emphasizing scale and primary question to keep assignments MECE.

subspecialties.name: Population Ecology
subspecialties.tagline: Dynamics of populations, demography, and life-history evolution
covers: Population size and growth, demography, density dependence
examples: age-structured models, life-table analyses, population viability analyses
explicitly_excludes: Community Ecology (multi-species interactions)
Evolutionary Biology (purely genetic evolution without ecological population processes)

subspecialties.name: Community Ecology
subspecialties.tagline: Interactions among species and community assembly processes
covers: Predation, competition, mutualism, trophic structure, assembly rules
examples: food-web studies, coexistence mechanisms, interaction networks
explicitly_excludes: Ecosystem Ecology (focus on energy/material flows rather than species interactions)
Invasion Biology (if applied to non-native species — use that slot)

subspecialties.name: Evolutionary Biology
subspecialties.tagline: Mechanisms and patterns of genetic and phenotypic evolution
covers: Natural selection, speciation, adaptation, macroevolutionary patterns
examples: experimental evolution, comparative trait evolution, theoretical evolution
explicitly_excludes: Phylogenetics (phylogeny reconstruction and methods)
Biology (molecular/genomic mechanism papers belong in Biology)

subspecialties.name: Behavioral Ecology
subspecialties.tagline: Ecological and evolutionary causes of animal behavior
covers: Foraging, mating systems, social behavior, behavioral strategies
examples: optimal foraging, sexual selection, social network studies
explicitly_excludes: Evolutionary Biology (if the paper is purely genetic/evolutionary modeling without behavior)
Neuroscience or Physiology (mechanistic neural work → Biology)

subspecialties.name: Macroecology
subspecialties.tagline: Large-scale patterns and laws of biodiversity and abundance
covers: Species–area relationships, scaling of diversity, global trait patterns
examples: latitudinal diversity gradients, body-size distributions, scaling theory
explicitly_excludes: Biogeography (if focused on spatial history and distribution drivers rather than emergent large-scale patterns)
Earth & Environmental (climate system–scale studies belong there)

subspecialties.name: Ecosystem Ecology
subspecialties.tagline: Energy, nutrient cycles, and functioning of ecosystems
covers: Primary production, decomposition, biogeochemical cycling, ecosystem services
examples: nutrient flux studies, productivity–decomposition coupling, carbon budgets at ecosystem scale
explicitly_excludes: Earth & Environmental (climate-scale or Earth-system modeling)
Microbial Ecology (if focused on microbes as agents, use Microbial Ecology)

subspecialties.name: Paleontology
subspecialties.tagline: Fossils, deep-time biodiversity, and evolutionary history
covers: Fossil description, macroevolutionary trends, extinction and origination dynamics
examples: fossil assemblage analyses, mass extinction studies, paleoecology
explicitly_excludes: Phylogenetics (if a paper is solely about molecular phylogenies of living taxa → Biology or Phylogenetics)
Earth & Environmental (paleoclimate modeling belongs there when focused on climate systems)

subspecialties.name: Phylogenetics
subspecialties.tagline: Tree reconstruction, comparative methods, and evolutionary history inference
covers: Phylogeny estimation, divergence dating, comparative trait methods
examples: methodological advances for tree inference, comparative tests of trait evolution
explicitly_excludes: Biology (sequence-level, genomics method papers belong to Biology)
Evolutionary Biology (conceptual evolutionary studies without phylogenetic analysis)

subspecialties.name: Biogeography
subspecialties.tagline: Spatial distributions and historical drivers of biodiversity
covers: Range evolution, dispersal, vicariance, historical assembly of regional biotas
examples: species distribution modeling, paleodistribution reconstructions, dispersal corridors
explicitly_excludes: Macroecology (if focus is on emergent patterns rather than dispersal/history)
Earth & Environmental (climate-driven range shifts when primarily about climate systems)

subspecialties.name: Conservation Biology
subspecialties.tagline: Biodiversity threats, protection strategies, and applied conservation science
covers: Threat assessment, protected areas, population recovery, policy-relevant ecology
examples: extinction risk analyses, conservation planning, human–wildlife conflict studies
explicitly_excludes: Restoration Ecology (active habitat restoration projects belong there)
Biology (molecular conservation genetics belongs in Biology)

subspecialties.name: Restoration Ecology
subspecialties.tagline: Active recovery and restoration of degraded ecosystems and habitats
covers: Restoration techniques, rewilding, ecosystem rehabilitation effectiveness
examples: restoration monitoring, revegetation experiments, functional recovery studies
explicitly_excludes: Conservation Biology (if the paper is about threat assessment or policy rather than active restoration)
Earth & Environmental (engineering-focused large-scale interventions may go there)

subspecialties.name: Landscape Ecology
subspecialties.tagline: Spatial structure, connectivity, and processes across landscapes
covers: Patch dynamics, habitat fragmentation, movement ecology at landscape scales
examples: connectivity modeling, land-use change impacts, spatially explicit population models
explicitly_excludes: Biogeography (if focus is historical distribution rather than contemporary landscape structure)
Earth & Environmental (landscape-scale climate/land system modeling belongs there)

subspecialties.name: Microbial Ecology
subspecialties.tagline: Roles of microbes in ecological processes and community interactions
covers: Microbial community ecology, microbe-mediated ecosystem processes, host–microbe interactions
examples: microbiome ecology, soil microbial function studies, microbial food webs
explicitly_excludes: Biology (molecular microbiology, genomics-heavy method papers belong to Biology)
Ecosystem Ecology (if the study addresses ecosystem fluxes primarily, use Ecosystem Ecology)

subspecialties.name: Invasion Biology
subspecialties.tagline: Ecology, impacts, and management of non-native species
covers: Mechanisms of invasion, impacts on native communities, management and policy
examples: invasion spread modeling, impact assessments, eradication/control studies
explicitly_excludes: Community Ecology (basic interaction theory unrelated to non-native status)
Conservation Biology (if framed as broader biodiversity threat/policy rather than invasion-focused)

uncovered_concerns: Genomics-heavy evolutionary work: many evolutionary papers use genomics; we route sequence/genomic-method papers to Biology — could be confusing for users expecting genetics inside Evolutionary Biology.
Modeling vs empirical split: theoretical/modeling work can cross multiple slots (macroecology, population, evolutionary); tags and abstracts must guide correct assignment.
Microbial ecology audience is small but distinct; careful exclusion language needed to avoid shunting microbe-driven ecosystem process papers into Ecosystem Ecology or Biology incorrectly.
